data.content_ref: inline:{"claim": "Sea Peoples invasions caused direct military destruction of palace centers, while Egypt's geographic depth and fortified Nile corridor enabled absorption of refugee waves and raiding parties without systemic disintegration", "mechanism": "Military invasion differential: linear coastal geography of palatial states enabled rapid sequential destruction, whereas Egypt's compact riparian geography with unified command allowed defensive in-depth attrition", "scope": {"covers": ["Mycenaean Greece", "Hittite Anatolia", "Ugarit", "Levantine city-states", "Egyptian survival"], "excludes": ["Mesopotamian continuities", "Iranian plateau developments", "Cyprus intermediary role"]}, "forbidden": [{"case": "Archaeological evidence shows Sea Peoples destruction layers post-date administrative collapse and abandonment at major sites", "eval": "rubric:std-hist"}, {"case": "Egyptian records describe Sea Peoples as settled mercenaries integrated into military rather than repelled invaders", "eval": "rubric:std-hist"}, {"case": "Hittite capital Hattusa shows no destruction layer but gradual abandonment", "eval": "rubric:std-hist"}], "prose_notes": "Reinventional of traditional invasion hypothesis with geographic framing; vulnerable to criticism that it reverses cause and effect if collapse preceded invasions"}
